options: Loss of small molecules like H₂O
Monomers joining without loss of any atom (chain growth)
explanation: Addition (chain growth) polymerisation: monomers with double/triple bonds join without loss of any byproduct. E.g., polyethylene from ethylene.
name: Addition Polymerisation

options: Loss of small molecules (like H₂O, HCl) at each step
explanation: Condensation (step growth) polymerisation: monomers react with loss of small molecules (H₂O, HCl). E.g., nylon, polyester, Bakelite.
name: Condensation Polymerisation

explanation: Nylon-6,6: condensation of hexamethylenediamine (H₂N(CH₂)₆NH₂) + adipic acid (HOOC(CH₂)₄COOH). Numbers refer to carbon atoms in each monomer.
name: Nylon-6,6

text: Bakelite is a thermosetting polymer formed by:
options: Condensation of phenol and formaldehyde (cross-linked network)
explanation: Bakelite: phenol + formaldehyde (H₂C=O) → cross-linked 3D network polymer. Thermosetting: cannot be remoulded once set.
name: Bakelite Formation

text: Buna-N (nitrile rubber) is a copolymer of:
explanation: Buna-N (NBR): copolymer of butadiene (CH₂=CH–CH=CH₂) and acrylonitrile (CH₂=CHCN). Oil-resistant synthetic rubber.
name: Buna-N Composition

text: The degree of polymerisation (DP) is defined as:
options: Number of monomer units in a polymer chain
explanation: Degree of polymerisation: DP = Molar mass of polymer / Molar mass of monomer. High DP → high molecular weight polymer.
name: Degree of Polymerisation

text: Natural rubber is a polymer of:
explanation: Natural rubber: cis-1,4-polyisoprene (from isoprene, CH₂=C(CH₃)–CH=CH₂). Gutta percha is trans-1,4-polyisoprene (hard).
name: Natural Rubber Monomer

options: Cross-linking rubber chains with sulfur at elevated temperature
explanation: Vulcanisation: rubber + S (1.5–5%) at 100–160°C → disulfide bridges (cross-links) between chains. Improves strength, elasticity, and reduces stickiness.
name: Vulcanisation

explanation: Teflon (polytetrafluoroethylene, PTFE): addition polymerisation of CF₂=CF₂. Highly chemically inert, used in non-stick cookware.
name: Teflon Monomer

options: Polyester formed from ethylene glycol + terephthalic acid
explanation: PET: condensation polymerisation of ethylene glycol (HOCH₂CH₂OH) + terephthalic acid (1,4-benzenedicarboxylic acid). Used in bottles, fibres (Dacron, Terylene).
name: PET Formation

text: Thermoplastics differ from thermosetting plastics in that they:
options: Can be softened on heating and reshaped (linear/branched chains)
explanation: Thermoplastics: linear or branched → soften on heating, can be remoulded and recycled (PVC, polyethylene, nylon). Thermosets: cross-linked → cannot be remoulded.
name: Thermoplastics vs Thermosets

text: Glyptal is a polyester used as paint/varnish, formed from:
explanation: Glyptal (alkyd resin): glycerol (triol) + phthalic anhydride → cross-linked polyester. Used in paints, lacquers.
name: Glyptal Polyester

text: PMMA (Plexiglas, Perspex) is a polymer of:
explanation: PMMA: addition polymerisation of methyl methacrylate. Transparent, used as glass substitute (Plexiglas). Also called acrylic glass.
name: PMMA Monomer

text: Nylon-6 is different from nylon-6,6 in that it is made from:
explanation: Nylon-6: ring-opening polymerisation of caprolactam (ε-caprolactam, 6-membered cyclic amide). Single monomer, unlike nylon-6,6 (two monomers).
name: Nylon-6 vs Nylon-6,6

text: Biodegradable polymers are preferred because they:
options: Can be broken down by microorganisms, reducing pollution
explanation: Biodegradable polymers (e.g., PHBV, PLA, nylon-2-nylon-6): degraded by microorganisms into CO₂, H₂O, and biomass. Reduce plastic pollution.
name: Biodegradable Polymers
